data.title: Spire outlines $11.2B decade-long capital plan and 5%–7% EPS growth target as Tennessee acquisition nears close
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Spire Inc. (SR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Scott Doyle, President and CEO, highlighted significant progress toward strategic goals, stating, "Adjusted EPS came in at $4.44, up 7.5% from $4.13 in fiscal 2024, reflecting growth across all segments, driven by infrastructure investments." He announced the pending acquisition of Piedmont Natural Gas Tennessee from Duke, with the deal expected to close in the first quarter of 2026. Doyle also welcomed Steve Greenley as Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer, emphasizing his 25 years of utility operations experience.

  • Doyle noted the company invested $922 million in fiscal 2025, with nearly 90% allocated to utilities, and underscored the passage of Missouri legislation allowing for a future test year rate setting model. "This new forward-looking approach will allow natural gas and water utilities to set rates based on projected costs rather than historical expenses, enabling prudent planning, attractive investments in energy infrastructure and fueling economic growth statewide."

  • The Board approved a 5.1% dividend increase to $3.30 per share, marking the 23rd consecutive year of dividend growth.

  • Adam Woodard, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "In fiscal 2025, we reported adjusted earnings of $275.5 million or $4.44 per share compared to $247.4 million or $4.13 per share in the prior year. These results include a fourth quarter adjusted loss of $24 million or $0.47 per share, reflecting the seasonality of our businesses."

Outlook

  • Doyle provided fiscal 2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $5.25 to $5.45, excluding the pending Piedmont Tennessee acquisition but including a full year of earnings from natural gas storage facilities. Fiscal 2027 adjusted EPS guidance was issued at $5.65 to $5.85, reflecting a full year of expected earnings from Piedmont Tennessee and excluding Spire Storage earnings due to the anticipated asset sale.

  • Long-term adjusted EPS growth guidance was reaffirmed at 5% to 7%, using the fiscal 2027 midpoint as a base. The 10-year capital plan, including Tennessee, totals $11.2 billion.

  • Management stated that by fiscal year 2030, Spire expects total rate base and capitalization to grow to $10.7 billion from an estimated $8.2 billion at the end of fiscal 2026.

Financial Results

  • Woodard reported that gas utilities earned $231 million, up almost 5% or over $10 million from last year, with growth partially offset by higher O&M and depreciation expenses. Midstream delivered $56 million, nearly $23 million higher than the previous year, driven by capacity and asset optimization at Spire Storage.

  • Gas marketing earned $26 million, an increase of $2.5 million, while other corporate costs rose to $38 million, reflecting higher interest expenses and the absence of a prior-year benefit from an interest rate hedge.

  • The updated capital plan totals $4.8 billion for fiscal 2026–2030, with 70% dedicated to safety and reliability projects and 19% for customer expansion and new business connections.

  • Corporate and Others are anticipated to be in the range of negative $31 million to negative $37 million for fiscal 2026, an improvement from last year's loss of $38 million.

Q&A

  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Asked for more detail on the long-term growth rate and expectations for earned ROEs. Doyle explained, "When we think about earned returns within the utility, particularly in Missouri, we're getting closer to our allowed returns in Missouri. We'll file another case in Missouri in the fall of next year...the outcome of that case is not going to be reflected in the FY '27 time period."

  • Zimbardo also asked about FFO to debt targets. Woodard replied, "We're at the bottom of the threshold ranges now but...we see a pretty steady movement up into the middle of the threshold bands for both Moody's and S&P going forward, really premised on the recoveries in Missouri."

  • Gabriel Moreen, Mizuho: Inquired about financing mix and timing. Woodard stated, "We continue to see -- feel confident about taking a very balanced mix of debt and equity...we are seeing quite a bit of interest" regarding the storage asset sale.

  • Moreen asked about O&M assumptions for the future. Doyle said, "O&M, our guide for this year is below the rate of inflation. And historically, that's been our guide year-over-year and that will be actually our performance this year was below the rate of inflation."

  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg: Asked if the future test year rate adjustment in 2028 could fall outside the 5% to 7% EPS growth range. Doyle indicated guidance is based on current knowledge and best estimates.

  • Fremont queried the likelihood of a storage asset sale. Woodard replied, "We have seen quite a bit of interest and -- strong interest in the assets, but more to come there."

  • Alexis Kania, BTIG: Asked about the mix in Midstream and implications for balance sheet targets if storage is sold. Woodard said the evaluation process is ongoing.

  • Selman Akyol, Stifel: Asked about dividend growth and long-term capital needs. Woodard responded, "We would continue to expect the dividend to grow basically at our earnings growth rate. And we do target the kind of the common payout ratio for utilities in that 55% to 65% range."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth rate sustainability, regulatory return trajectories, financing, and capital allocation, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for clarification and details on execution.

  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing stability, prudent planning, and the successful progress of strategic initiatives. Doyle consistently expressed confidence: "We're confident in our ability to deliver sustainable value for our customers, communities and shareholders in the years ahead."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remains steadily inquisitive, while management’s tone has shifted from cautious optimism to firmer confidence amid visible regulatory and operational progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fiscal 2026 and 2027 EPS guidance are new developments, along with detailed capital planning and the anticipated completion of the Piedmont Tennessee acquisition.

  • There is a greater focus on constructive regulatory frameworks, especially with Missouri's future test year legislation and the transition in Alabama’s RSE mechanism.

  • Analysts again concentrated on earnings quality, regulatory recovery, and capital allocation, but engaged more on the specifics of financing and the impact of asset sales this quarter.

  • Management’s confidence and clarity on strategic direction and financial targets have strengthened versus the previous quarter, reflecting successful execution and clearer regulatory outcomes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged higher utility O&M expense as a drag on quarterly results.

  • The storage asset sale process remains uncertain; Woodard noted the ongoing evaluation and significant market interest.

  • Rate case outcomes and the successful integration of the Tennessee acquisition remain key execution risks.

  • Regulatory adaptation to new test year models in Missouri and maintaining credit metrics during acquisition financing were highlighted.

Final Takeaway

Spire's management underscored robust execution on operational and financial priorities, marked by EPS growth, regulatory advancements, and a disciplined capital plan. The company is targeting significant EPS expansion through 2027, backed by a $11.2 billion capital plan and the pending Tennessee acquisition, while maintaining a long-term adjusted EPS growth rate of 5% to 7%. The focus on a balanced financing approach, dividend growth, and stable regulatory environments positions Spire to deliver sustainable value for stakeholders as it navigates integration and investment opportunities ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: KKR's Janney Montgomery Scott nears sale of financial-services unit - report
data.description:
KKR-owned (KKR) Janney Montgomery Scott is in advanced discussions to sell its institutional equities business and its unit that advises depository institutions, according to a media report on Friday.

Janney is seeking to shed the businesses to Brean Capital, to exit capital markets and shift its focus on wealth management, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The potential deal would include a group of investment bankers who advise financial institutions on M&A and raising capital, along with its equity research and equity sales and trading units, the people said.

While the talks are in an advanced stage, the deal could still unravel, they told Bloomberg.

Earlier this month, Janney agreed to sell some other capital markets units to Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), including its M&A advisory businesses, public-finance group, and fixed-income sales and trading, Bloomberg had previously reported.

data.title: From pandemic to e-commerce disruption: How McMillon evolved Walmart through the years
data.description:
Walmart (WMT) CEO Doug McMillon will step down in January next year after being in the role for over a decade. 

McMillon took the reins of the company in 2014, a time when Walmart (WMT) was facing increasing competition from its digital-first rivals. It was a challenging time, as the retail giant's stock seemed to be producing only modest gains and the business was losing momentum due to the advent of online shopping. 

He has led Walmart to incredible success over the decade, quadrupling the company’s market cap during his tenure and driving shares up 400% over the last 10 years, double the performance of the S&P 500.

McMillon started his Walmart career in 1984 as an hourly associate, picking orders and unloading trailers in a warehouse, according to his LinkedIn.

The company has tapped John Furner to succeed McMillon as the company’s new chief executive, effective February 1, 2026.

The way ahead was rough for McMillon, yet he headed into the challenge head-on, even though the early periods were difficult.

He also has seen a massive amount of success as compared to his predecessors when it comes to the stock performance of Walmart (WMT).

Under H. Lee Scott, WMT stock's return was -26.9%; under Mike Duke, it was +60.4%; and under McMillon, it has been a whopping +323.4% so far.

The Early Years

After a modest gain of 9.1% in 2014, Walmart’s (WMT) shares plunged almost 29% in 2015—one of the worst years in its modern history—as the company poured billions into wages, store upgrades, and e-commerce. Profitability slipped, and investors questioned whether the retailer could catch up to its online rival Amazon ((AMZN)).

Walmart (WMT) under McMillon dived deep into expanding its online presence. In 2015 he had aggressively started increasing its online grocery shopping services with launches in new markets. At the time, WMT also went for a different strategy. The company combined online grocery shopping with a local pickup option at some stores across the U.S.

The Turning Point 

But 2016 and especially 2017 marked a turning point. As online grocery rolled out and store operations improved, the stock was up 12.7% in 2016 and surged more than 40% in 2017 alone, reflecting renewed conviction in Walmart’s ability to modernize at scale.

In 2018, the stock did fall 5.67%. In 2018, WMT continued its push into online strategy. A CNBC report said the retailer had plans to expand its online grocery delivery service to about 800 stores by the end of that year.

But in the next two years, the firm staged a rebound. In 2019 Walmart (WMT) rose 27.6%.

The COVID-19 years 

Walmart (WMT) under McMillon seemed resilient during the pandemic. In 2021, it churned out total revenues of ~$559 billion, up from ~$524 billion in 2020. It beat the COVID-19 slump, and its stock rose 21.3% in 2020. 

In 2020 Walmart also launched the Walmart+ membership program. 

The rise in revenue in 2021 was mainly driven by heightened demand in groceries, household essentials, and curbside services. 

Walmart (WMT) also absorbed roughly $4 billion in pandemic-related costs, including safety measures and wage increases. Rising labor expenses and supply chain disruptions pressured margins, and investors grew cautious even as sales climbed.

However, its stock seemed to stagnate in 2021 and was only up 0.4% in the year. 

Inflation worries and the turnaround

Inflation weighed heavily on consumer behavior in 2022. Higher inventory levels, cost inflation, and a shift in consumer spending patterns created additional strain. The stock fell 2% in the year.

Profit, however, continued to improve in 2022, climbing to more than $25 billion, even as Walmart absorbed higher labor expenses, supply chain disruptions, and inflationary pressures.

These shifts underscored Walmart’s (WMT) strategic approach under McMillon: prioritizing long-term competitiveness—automation, digital expansion, and improved store operations—while navigating a volatile macro environment that kept margins under pressure.

The turnaround came in 2023, with the stock rising 11.2%. However, it was last year that WMT had a breakout period, as its shares surged 71.9% in 2024.

Operating income climbed to $27.01 billion in 2024 and then further to around $29.35 billion in 2025, marking its strongest profit performance of McMillon’s tenure. 

For fiscal 2024, the increase was mainly driven by the Walmart U.S. segment. The rise was also helped by growing contributions from higher-margin businesses like advertising and membership services.

Under McMillon, Walmart (WMT) transformed from a traditional brick-and-mortar chain into a dominant omnichannel retailer.

He steered the company through a decade defined by digital disruption, global inflation, a pandemic, and a shifting competitive landscape.

How Walmart's stock has performed since McMillon became the CEO:

data.title: Enanta rises as J.P. Morgan starts at Overweight on RSV therapy
data.description:
Shares of Enanta Pharmaceuticals traded higher on Friday after two straight sessions of losses as J.P. Morgan launched its coverage with an Overweight recommendation, citing the potential of zelicapavir, an experimental therapy developed by the biotech against the respiratory syncytial virus ((RSV)).

Analyst Anupam Rama issued a December 2026 price target of $17 per share for the antiviral developer, projecting more than $1.5B in peak global sales for zelicapavir, including more than $1B from the U.S. alone.

His bullish view came after ENTA surged more than 90% in September following topline data from a mid-stage trial for the oral, once-daily therapy in high-risk outpatient adults with acute RSV.

“Importantly, we see zelicapavir as strategically attractive on the back of the strong phase 2b data and long-term market potential,” Rama wrote, arguing that the readout has derisked a potential Phase 3 trial in high-risk adults, which is expected to begin after FDA feedback.

“Our sense is that Enanta is focused on partnering the product out—i.e., a potential source of significant non-dilutive financing,” the analyst added, noting, however, that subject to a partnership, peak net global revenue from zelicapavir could reach more than $300M.

data.title: Candel pausing development of CAN-2409 in prostate cancer type
data.description:
• Candel Therapeutics (CADL) said it is pausing the development of CAN-2409 specifically for an indication in borderline resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma, where it is in phase 2 testing.
  • The company said that it wants to focus the asset in localized, intermediate/high risk pancreatic cancer and non-small lung cancer. Developed on an adenovirus platform, CAN-2409 is ready for a BLA submission in the former indication and in phase 2 for the latter.
  • Candel noted a phase 3 study in NSCLC is slated to begin in Q2 2026.
  • The biopharma's Q3 financial results, released on Thursday, missed on the bottom line.

data.title: NYC mayor-elect Mamdani calls for solidarity with striking Starbucks baristas
data.description:
New York City mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani is calling for New Yorkers to boycott Starbucks (SBUX) to send a “powerful message” to the Seattle coffee chain after some of its baristas walked off the job.

“While workers are on strike, I won’t be buying any Starbucks, and I’m asking you to join us,” Mamdani posted on X. “Together, we can send a powerful message: No contract, no coffee.”

On Thursday, 12,000 unionized Starbucks (SBUX) employees across the U.S. went on strike coinciding with Red Cup Day, the company’s annual holiday promotion. The Starbucks Workers Unite union initiated the strike to address “stonewalling” by Starbucks management pertaining to a new contract providing higher take-home pay, better hours, and address numerous claims of unfair labor practices.  

As the striking employees represent less than 4% of Starbucks’ (SBUX) workforce, the stoppage is unlikely to adversely affect the company’s operations.

However, NYC currently has the most Starbucks’ (SBUX) restaurants than any other U.S. city with roughly 200 locations across Manhattan. While it’s unlikely New York’s newly elected socialist mayor will mobilize all New Yorkers against the coffee chain, a protracted strike could amplify reputational pressure in one of its most visible markets.

Consequently, Mamdani’s post weighed on shares and drove SBUX to its session low of $84.55.

data.title: Saudi wealth fund PIF slashes Q3 stakes, keeps Uber, EA, few others
data.description:
Saudi Arabia's sovereign wealth fund unloaded most of its stock holdings in Q3 2025, while holding on to Uber Technologies (UBER), Lucid Group (LCID), Electronic Arts (EA), Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO) and Claritev (CTEV), according to a U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission filing on Friday.

Among the higher-profile names the Public Investment Fund exited during the three-month period include Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Bank of America (BAC), Broadcom (AVGO), Coca-Cola (KO), Home Depot (HD), Eli Lilly (LLY), Nvidia (NVDA), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), UnitedHealth (UNH), Prologis (PLD) and Netflix (NFLX).

The firm did not make any new buys during the quarter, and the positions PIF kept were untouched, the 13F filing showed.

data.title: UBS spotlights 30 disrupters for the next decade
data.description:
UBS has unveiled a curated list of 30 companies it believes are best positioned to thrive over the coming decade, emphasizing that the next wave of market leadership will be driven by technology-enabled disruption across multiple industries.

In an investor note, the firm argues that the most significant opportunities through 2030 will emerge from greentech, fintech, and healthtech, along with the broader universe of enabling technologies such as artificial intelligence and cybersecurity. These areas, UBS noted, are poised to generate the innovations that redefine traditional business models and reshape competitive dynamics. 

According to the investment bank, companies leading these transformative segments are likely to outpace the broader market in earnings growth as long-term, structural tailwinds—ranging from digital adoption to sustainability-focused investment—continue to accelerate. 

Outlined below are the 30 names ranked by year-to-date performance: 

No. 1: Societe Generale (OTCPK:SCGLY) +138.3%

No. 2: Rolls-Royce Holdings (OTCPK:RYCEY) +105.5%

data.title: Guardian Capital Group GAAP EPS of $2.89, revenue of $91.96M
data.description:
• Guardian Capital Group  press release (GCG:CA): Q3 GAAP EPS of $2.89.
  • Revenue of $91.96M (-6.3% Y/Y).

data.title: Molina nabs Florida Medicaid contract
data.description:
• Molina Healthcare (MOH) was awarded a contract from the Florida Agency for Health Care Administration to provide coverage to about 120,000 enrollees in the Statewide Medicaid Managed Care and Children's Health Insurance Programs.
  • Enrollees are part of the state's Title XIX and Title XXI Children’s Medical Services Program for those with special healthcare needs. 
  • Molina noted that in 2025, total premiums for the program are expected to be ~$5B.
